topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into a perfectly curated afternoon. My thumb hovered over the link, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was the listing for 41 Willow Creek Lane. My childhood home. A wave of sharp, unpleasant heat rushed up my neck. I reread the details, the professional photographs of the overgrown garden and the familiar porch swing, and a bitter taste coated my mouth. How dare someone else be looking at the place I grew up? I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, staring out at the street, feeling a sudden, profound sense of being exposed, as if the very foundation of my past had been carelessly laid bare for public consumption.

[story 2]

"No way," I muttered, shaking my head as I stared at the property details. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. This wasn't just a house; it was the backdrop to every scraped knee, every whispered secret. Seeing it cataloged, priced, and available for purchase felt like a deliberate erasure of my history. I paced the length of my living room, my steps quick and agitated. I ran a hand through my hair, the gesture jerky and frustrated. The sheer audacity of it—someone else claiming ownership over that slice of my memory—made my chest feel tight, constricted by a rising tide of resentment.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my chair, the screen illuminating my face with a harsh, unforgiving light. The listing for my old neighborhood house was undeniable, a digital monument to a life I thought was entirely mine. A slow, simmering anger began to bubble up, warm and corrosive. I didn't move immediately; instead, I just stared, my gaze fixed on the listing details, my breathing shallow and uneven. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, almost too quiet. "They're selling it," I managed, the words tasting like ash. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing to physically distance myself from the sight, the energy radiating off me a tense, coiled spring ready to snap.
